entries.finding: Tempo now ≈2:1 ✓ (was ~1:1 — the One-Two drill is landing). Path straight (SBST) on BOTH putters — matches Journey Session 4. Face square through impact, no visible toe-over. Balls start dead on the center line. Early lift can't be judged from overhead.

faults.why: unverified this session — overhead can't see it; film face-on to confirm the fix

entries.text: Film 2–3 strokes face-on + DTL — verify the early-lift fix and the calmer release seen in Session 4

entries.setup: 5 clips · overhead · 3× Phantom 7.5 + 2× Newport 2 · indoor track mat
entries.finding: Tempo now ≈2:1 ✓ (was ~1:1 — the One-Two drill is landing). Path straight (SBST) on BOTH putters — matches Journey Session 4. Face square through impact, no visible toe-over. Balls start dead on the center line. Early lift can't be judged from overhead.

entries.setup: Baseline re-analysis · batch 1 of old clips · 1 overhead + 4 ground-level DTL · Phantom 7.5
entries.finding: EARLY LIFT confirmed on film: head rises steeply off the mat in the backstroke and hangs visibly high after impact — the 'up & out' before-picture is now documented. Old backswing ≈0.40s vs ≈0.65s in the new clips: a ~60% longer, slower load. Path was straight even then. Note: frame counts read the old tempo nearer 2:1 than the logged ~1:1 — the measurable change is the longer load, not the ratio.

entries.setup: Baseline re-analysis · all 10 v1/v2 clips · overhead + ground-level DTL + side views · Phantom 7.5
entries.finding: EARLY LIFT fully documented from three angles: steep rise in the backstroke, head hanging high after impact — in the side view it exits the frame upward. Old backswing ≈0.40s vs ≈0.65s in Session 4: a ~60% longer load. Path straight and starts on-line in every old overhead — the stroke's bones were always good. Face-closing was too subtle to confirm or refute at analysis frame rate. This is the yardstick your next face-on film gets measured against.

entries.text: BUY (decision locked): zero-torque at 34", lie set flat — Mezz.1 Max → Odyssey S2S → Spider ZT. Pick with the 10-ball 5-footer test at the shop

detail.story: Frame-extracted at 15fps and read frame-by-frame. The backswing now runs about 0.65 seconds against a 0.30-second downstroke — right at the 2:1 target, and a visibly longer, calmer load than the baseline. The head tracked the mat lines straight back and through on the Phantom and the Newport 2 alike, the wings stayed symmetric to the line through the hitting zone (no visible toe-over), and every ball started dead on the center line.
detail.compare: Versus the baseline clips: backswing load grew from ≈0.40s to ≈0.65s (~60% longer). The face-closing logged in Session 1 did not appear on any rep. The left miss did not appear on camera.
detail.limits: Overhead cannot see vertical head movement, so the early-lift fix is still unverified — that is what the face-on film request is for. Small face rotations can hide inside motion blur at 15fps.

detail.story: All ten v1/v2 clips re-analyzed with the same frame-extraction method as Session 4, giving measured numbers on both ends of the comparison. The early lift is documented from three separate angles: the head rises steeply off the mat during the short backstroke, passes low at impact, then climbs immediately — in the side view it exits the top of the frame on the follow-through. The backswing measured ≈0.40s: short, steep and brisk.
detail.compare: One honest disagreement with the journey: frame counts read the old back-to-through ratio nearer 2:1 than the logged ~1:1. What actually screamed 'quick and hit-y' was the short, steep stroke overall — the measured improvement is the longer, lower, more deliberate load, not the ratio.
detail.limits: No true face-on clip exists in the archive, so lift height couldn't be measured in inches — the side view is the best available yardstick until the next face-on session.

entries.setup: 5 clips · eye-level overhead · L.A.B. DF3i — first filmed reps
entries.finding: Start line: dead on the center line, all 5 reps — no left pull anywhere. Tempo measured 0.55s back / 0.23s down ≈ 2.4:1 (motion analysis, steadiest clip). The stroke changes are holding with the new putter in the bag.

metrics.n: tracked the dashed line every rep

detail.story: First filmed strokes with the DF3i, shot from an eye-level overhead with your feet in frame. Every one of the five balls started dead on the mat's center line and tracked it until it left the frame — the miss the whole project was built around simply did not appear. Tempo was measured objectively on the steadiest clip using frame-to-frame motion analysis: quiet address, takeaway, a clear transition dip, and an impact spike gave 0.55s back and 0.23s down — about 2.4:1, squarely in the tour window. The follow-through consistently finishes with the head over the line and holds.
detail.compare: Versus Session 4 (Phantom): tempo holding at ~2:1+ (0.55s vs 0.65s load — slightly brisker but the ratio is intact). Versus baseline: a different stroke entirely. The zero-torque head now guarantees the square face that used to depend on timing.
detail.limits: Four of five clips were handheld with visible sway, which limited motion analysis to the steadiest clip. This angle still can't see vertical lift — the face-on film remains the last open item. Next session: prop the phone against something instead of holding it.

entries.text: Session 5 filmed ✓ (overhead, DF3i) — remaining: face-on clips for the lift verdict + the 20-ball 5-footer baseline

briefing.focus: Fairway-finder day. 139 slope, narrow and tree-lined — the mini driver and 2-iron are your scoring clubs off the tee, and bogey is never an emergency here.

sections.b: Mungeam design (2004) carved into a hillside: 6,935 yards, par 71, rating 73.7 / slope 139 from the tips — the longest, toughest, best-kept muni in Westchester. Narrow fairways lined with trees and fescue, elevated tees AND elevated greens, a few blind shots, and almost no flat lies. Front nine is an accuracy exam; back nine wants length and short game. Play the tees that match a 6,300–6,500 game, not the tips — the slope is real.

sections.t: Off the tee — your bag is built for this place
sections.b: This is exactly why you carry the r7 Mini and the 2-iron. Blind or squeezed landing area (the 2nd is famously tight, the 7th is a beast): mini driver (~220) or 2-iron (~205) and walk to the fairway. Save the Stealth 2 for the open, longer back-nine holes where the extra 15 yards matters. One rule all day: on any tee shot you can't fully see, take the club you'd bet $100 finds short grass.

sections.t: The lies are the hidden test
sections.b: Hillside course = sidehill everything. Ball above your feet wants to go LEFT — with your strong lofts, take one less club and aim right edge. Ball below feet pushes right; widen the stance and swing smooth. Uphill approach: club UP, always — elevated greens reject anything short and roll it 20 yards back down the hill. Short-siding yourself here is the double-bogey machine; center of every green is a genuinely good shot.

When you punch out or lay up, do it to YOUR numbers: 95 (full 56°) or 108 (full 50°) — not 'as far as possible' into an awkward half-shot from a sloped lie. On uphill wedges into elevated greens, the ball lands steep and stops fast: fly it to the flag.

sections.t: Greens — first real test for the DF3i
sections.b: Reviewers call them tour-fast. Perfect debut for the zero-torque: your job is speed only, the face is handled. Lag ladder mindset from 25+ feet — die everything into the 6-foot circle. Spend the first three greens calibrating pace, and on breaking putts play extra break with dying speed. Keep the low-through-impact feel from the gate drill: that is still your one open fault, and fast greens punish a popped stroke worst.

Extremely hilly — take the cart, save the legs for the back nine. July heat means carries play a touch longer (load the weather tile at the first tee — the ladder's Today column does the math). Target: doubles are the enemy, not bogeys. Count them; fewer than three doubles on a 139 slope is a won day. Log the round in Coach after — score, putts, and what bit you — and I'll debrief it against this plan.

entries.setup: FULL SWING · 2 clips · simulator bay · screen-recorded
entries.finding: First long-game film on record: full coiled shoulder turn, unhurried ~3:1 tempo, and a balanced held finish — with the visible tracer running straight up the middle. No sign of the putting lift habit leaking into the full swing.

metrics.n: back to target at the top

detail.story: Two simulator-bay swings, captured as screen-recorded story clips. The good stuff is structural and it's all there: a genuinely full shoulder coil at the top (not an arms lift), an unhurried transition — the backswing takes roughly three times the downswing, which is right where a full swing should live — and a committed, balanced finish that holds while the ball flies. The one readable tracer runs straight up the middle of the fairway. Notably, the up-and-out habit from the putting baseline does not appear here: the follow-through wraps full and low.
detail.compare: First full-swing entry, so this IS the baseline. The putting project's faults look putting-specific — encouraging for the long game.
detail.limits: Screen-recorded social clips: heavy compression, UI overlays, and one camera angle per swing. Club face, path, plane, and strike location can't be judged from this. For a real swing breakdown, film direct slo-mo (240fps) — down-the-line at hand height and face-on — and send the raw clips.

entries.text: For a real swing breakdown: film 3 swings slo-mo, down-the-line at hand height + face-on, raw clips (not screen recordings)
